data science II midterm project

Muxin Li, Xinyi Yao, Yumeng Lu

2026-03-24

library(tidyverse)
library(mgcv)
library(patchwork)
library(caret) 
library(glmnet)
library(earth)
## Warning: package 'plotrix' was built under R version 4.5.2
library(pdp)
theme_set(
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
)

plot_labs = list(
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2),
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE),
  labs(x = NULL, y = "log(PFS)")
)

pretty_table = function(df, caption = NULL) {
  knitr::kable(df, caption = caption, align = "c") |>
    kableExtra::kable_styling(full_width = FALSE, bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"))
}

(I)

load("datA.RData")

datA= datA|>
  mutate(
    menopause = factor(menopause),
    race = factor(race),
    tumor_grade = factor(tumor_grade),
    ER = factor(ER),
    PR = factor(PR),
    HER2 = factor(HER2),
    stage = factor(stage),
    surgery = factor(surgery),
    chemo = factor(chemo),
    hormonal_therapy = factor(hormonal_therapy)
  )

GAM model

Step 1: choose linear terms and nonlinear terms briefly.

Menopause, race, tumor_grade, ER, PR, HER2, stage, surgery, chemo, hormonal_therapy are categorical variables. Usually, we can treat as linear terms. For age, bmi, tumor_size, lymph_nodes, and Ki67, we need to find whether they are linear or nonlinear.

p1 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= age, y= log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Age")
p2 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= bmi, y= log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "BMI")
p3 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= tumor_size, y= log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Tumor Size")
p4 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= lymph_nodes, y= log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Lymph Nodes")
p5 = ggplot(datA, aes(x= Ki67, y= log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Ki67")

(p1 | p2) / (p3 | p4) / p5 +
  plot_annotation(title = "Plots for Selecting Linear and Nonlinear Terms in Data A")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Comment: From the plot we find age is linear; lymph_nodes is linear; bmi has a clear curve (first rising and then falling); tumor_size and Ki67 are slightly nonlinear.

Step 2: choose smooth term exactly.

We are not sure tumor_size and Ki67 are smoothing terms, so we use ANOVA to compare.

m1 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

m2 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data =datA
)

m_tumor = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

m_ki67 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

anova(m1, m2, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                              
## 2    1974.7     952.34 1.4481   1.6994 2.4353 0.1047
anova(m1, m_tumor, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                              
## 2    1974.7     952.34 1.4481   1.6994 2.4353 0.1047
anova(m1, m_ki67, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev         Df    Deviance F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                                
## 2    1976.1     954.04 7.4776e-09 -1.3358e-10

Comment: Although exploratory plots suggested mild nonlinearity for tumor_size and Ki67, model comparisons using ANOVA indicate that smoothing terms do not significantly improve model fit. In particular, the smoothing term for Ki67 has effectively zero degrees of freedom, suggesting a linear relationship. Thus, both variables are retained as linear terms.

Step 3: summary the model and simplify.

gam_fit1= gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage +
    surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)
summary(gam_fit1)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.7210154  0.1110669  24.499  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.0041846  0.0019153  -2.185 0.029024 *  
## tumor_size        -0.0617453  0.0198687  -3.108 0.001913 ** 
## lymph_nodes       -0.0659591  0.0097351  -6.775 1.63e-11 ***
## Ki67              -0.0038725  0.0019844  -1.951 0.051143 .  
## menopause1         0.0396511  0.0468592   0.846 0.397557    
## race2              0.0372369  0.0454275   0.820 0.412487    
## race3              0.0641768  0.0561219   1.144 0.252960    
## race4             -0.0388745  0.0481849  -0.807 0.419892    
## tumor_grade2      -0.0453329  0.0463990  -0.977 0.328677    
## tumor_grade3      -0.1633398  0.0593064  -2.754 0.005938 ** 
## ER1                0.2344966  0.0515997   4.545 5.84e-06 ***
## PR1               -0.0048347  0.0387555  -0.125 0.900735    
## HER21             -0.1717877  0.0386554  -4.444 9.32e-06 ***
## stage2             0.0543338  0.0519110   1.047 0.295378    
## stage3             0.0931217  0.1030664   0.904 0.366364    
## surgery1          -0.0090098  0.0332095  -0.271 0.786187    
## chemo1            -0.0002041  0.0324330  -0.006 0.994980    
## hormonal_therapy1  0.1338895  0.0397960   3.364 0.000782 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##          edf Ref.df     F p-value    
## s(bmi) 3.905  4.883 7.211 2.8e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.213   Deviance explained = 22.2%
## GCV = 0.48814  Scale est. = 0.48255   n = 2000

Comment: Ki67, menopause, race, tumor_grade2, PR, stage, surgery, chemo are not statistically significant and could be excluded from the model. The model explained 21.3% of the variability of the outcome (R^2= 0.213).

gam_fit2 = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

summary(gam_fit2)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
##     ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.616320   0.090610  28.875  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.002942   0.001343  -2.190 0.028622 *  
## tumor_size        -0.049378   0.012886  -3.832 0.000131 ***
## lymph_nodes       -0.063182   0.008538  -7.400 1.99e-13 ***
## tumor_grade2      -0.074679   0.042897  -1.741 0.081857 .  
## tumor_grade3      -0.228148   0.045541  -5.010 5.93e-07 ***
## ER1                0.260201   0.042756   6.086 1.39e-09 ***
## HER21             -0.174936   0.038492  -4.545 5.83e-06 ***
## hormonal_therapy1  0.136634   0.039529   3.457 0.000559 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##          edf Ref.df     F p-value    
## s(bmi) 3.885  4.859 7.067   4e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.214   Deviance explained = 21.9%
## GCV = 0.48529  Scale est. = 0.48217   n = 2000
anova(gam_fit1,gam_fit2, test= "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
##     ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev      Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1976.1     954.04                               
## 2    1986.1     958.12 -10.024  -4.0845 0.8445 0.5858

Comment: Although the reduced model has slightly higher residual deviance, the difference is not statistically significant (p = 0.5858). This suggests that the excluded variables do not contribute meaningfully to model performance. Therefore, the simpler model is preferred, achieving a better balance between model fit and complexity. We choose gam_fit2 as final gam model.

plot(gam_fit2)
title("Estimated Smooth Effect of BMI in the Final GAM for Data A")

LM model

Step 1: build the LM model

lm_fit= lm(
  log_PFS ~  age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)

summary(lm_fit)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + 
##     tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy, data = datA)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.82120 -0.48336  0.01155  0.46708  2.73052 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.579825   0.124840  20.665  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.002880   0.001354  -2.127 0.033532 *  
## bmi                0.001406   0.003462   0.406 0.684597    
## tumor_size        -0.051536   0.012989  -3.968 7.52e-05 ***
## lymph_nodes       -0.061792   0.008604  -7.182 9.69e-13 ***
## tumor_grade2      -0.078086   0.043245  -1.806 0.071118 .  
## tumor_grade3      -0.228888   0.045920  -4.985 6.75e-07 ***
## ER1                0.260743   0.043093   6.051 1.72e-09 ***
## HER21             -0.172948   0.038802  -4.457 8.77e-06 ***
## hormonal_therapy1  0.138938   0.039830   3.488 0.000497 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7003 on 1990 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2041, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2005 
## F-statistic:  56.7 on 9 and 1990 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Step 2: compare with GAM model

set.seed(123)

folds = createFolds(datA$log_PFS, k = 10, list = TRUE)

cv_mse_lm  = numeric(10)
cv_mse_gam = numeric(10)

for(i in 1:10) {
  valid_idx = folds[[i]]
  
  cv_train = datA[-valid_idx, ]
  cv_valid = datA[valid_idx, ]
  
  lm_cv = lm(
    log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes +
      tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
    data = cv_train
  )
  
  gam_cv = gam(
    log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes +
      tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
    data = cv_train
  )
  
  pred_lm  = predict(lm_cv, newdata = cv_valid)
  pred_gam = predict(gam_cv, newdata = cv_valid)
  
  cv_mse_lm[i]  = mean((cv_valid$log_PFS - pred_lm)^2)
  cv_mse_gam[i] = mean((cv_valid$log_PFS - pred_gam)^2)
}

mse_lm= mean(cv_mse_lm)
mse_lm
## [1] 0.4930619
mse_gam= mean(cv_mse_gam)
mse_gam
## [1] 0.4857952

Comment: Under 10-fold cross-validation, the GAM model achieved a slightly lower mean squared error (0.486) compared to the linear model (0.493), indicating improved predictive performance. This suggests that modeling bmi with a nonlinear smooth term provides a modest benefit.

Penalized Regression Model

Step 1: find the x and y

x = model.matrix(
  log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 +
    menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 +
    stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA
)[, -1]

y = datA$log_PFS

Step 2: build Lasso and Ridge

set.seed(123)

cv_lasso= cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 1)

plot(cv_lasso)
title("Lasso Cross-Validation Curve", line = 3)

cv_lasso$lambda.min
## [1] 0.01401631
cv_ridge = cv.glmnet(x, y, alpha = 0)

plot(cv_ridge)
title("Ridge Cross-Validation Curve", line = 3)

Step 3: compare with other model

pred_lasso = predict(cv_lasso, x, s = "lambda.min")
pred_ridge = predict(cv_ridge, x, s = "lambda.min")

mse_lasso = mean((y - pred_lasso)^2)
mse_ridge = mean((y - pred_ridge)^2)

mse_lasso
## [1] 0.4882188
mse_ridge
## [1] 0.487001

Comment: Among the penalized regression models, Ridge achieved a slightly lower MSE (0.487) than Lasso (0.488), indicating marginally better predictive performance. For mse: GAM < Ridge < Lasso < LM.

MARS Model

Step 1: find the best model

ctrl1 = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10)

mars_grid = expand.grid(
  degree = 1:3,
  nprune = 2:15
)

set.seed(123)

mars_fit = train(
  log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datA,
  method = "earth",
  tuneGrid = mars_grid,
  trControl = ctrl1
)

ggplot(mars_fit) +
  labs(title = "MARS Cross-Validation Performance")

mars_fit$bestTune
##    nprune degree
## 10     11      1
coef(mars_fit$finalModel)
##       (Intercept)  h(6-lymph_nodes)               ER1       h(bmi-23.9) 
##        2.09077991        0.07748798        0.22755947       -0.02110819 
##       h(23.9-bmi)      h(Ki67-33.8)             HER21 hormonal_therapy1 
##       -0.05003047        0.02610809       -0.18465081        0.13632405 
##      tumor_grade3 h(tumor_size-0.4)      h(Ki67-24.1) 
##       -0.11666085       -0.04882760       -0.01789249

Comment: The optimal MARS model has degree = 1, indicating that no interaction terms are needed and the model is additive. The best model has 11 basis functions.

Step 2 : get mse to compare with other model

best_rmse_mars = mars_fit$results |>
  dplyr::filter(
    nprune == mars_fit$bestTune$nprune,
    degree == mars_fit$bestTune$degree
  ) |>
  dplyr::pull(RMSE)

best_mse_mars = best_rmse_mars^2
best_mse_mars
## [1] 0.4948591

Comment: To compare with other model, we choose MSE. We find MSE for MARS is 0.4948591.

Different model MSE

model_mse_a = data.frame(
  Model = c("LM", "GAM", "Ridge", "Lasso", "MARS"),
  MSE = c(mse_lm, mse_gam, mse_ridge, mse_lasso, best_mse_mars))|>
  arrange(MSE)

pretty_table(model_mse_a, caption = "Model Comparison by MSE for Data A")
Model Comparison by MSE for Data A
Model MSE
GAM 0.4857952
Ridge 0.4870010
Lasso 0.4882188
LM 0.4930619
MARS 0.4948591

Comment: For MSE comparison among five different models, GAM < Ridge < Lasso < LM < MARS. In this analysis, the generalized additive model (GAM) achieved the lowest mean squared error, indicating that modeling smooth nonlinear relationships—particularly for BMI—provides the greatest improvement in prediction accuracy. Ridge and Lasso regression offered modest improvements over the linear model by stabilizing coefficients and performing variable selection, but their gains were limited. In contrast, the MARS model, which captures nonlinearity through piecewise linear functions, performed worse than the GAM, suggesting that the underlying relationships are smooth rather than threshold-based.

(II)

Evaluate prediction model on dataset B

load("datB.RData")
datB = datB |>
  mutate(across(c(menopause, race, tumor_grade, ER, PR, HER2, 
                  stage, surgery, chemo, hormonal_therapy), factor))

# External Validation: Apply Model A to Data B
pred_B_ext = predict(gam_fit2, newdata = datB)

mse_ext = mean((datB$log_PFS - pred_B_ext)^2)
rmse_ext = sqrt(mse_ext)
r2_ext = cor(datB$log_PFS, pred_B_ext)^2

# Internal Validation: Build New Model for Data B
# Using the same GAM structure to ensure comparability
gam_fit_B = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
    ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datB
)

summary_B = summary(gam_fit_B)
r2_B = summary_B$r.sq

# Comparison Table
mse_comparison = data.frame(
  `Data Set` = c("Data A", "Data B"),
  MSE = c(best_mse_mars, mse_ext))
coef_A = as.data.frame(summary(gam_fit2)$p.coeff)
coef_B = as.data.frame(summary_B$p.coeff)
comparison_coefs = cbind(coef_A, coef_B)
colnames(comparison_coefs) = c("Beta_1990s", "Beta_2000s")

pretty_table(mse_comparison, caption = "External Validation MSE Comparison")
External Validation MSE Comparison
Data.Set MSE
Data A 0.4948591
Data B 0.7057918 
pretty_table(comparison_coefs, caption = "Coefficient Comparison Between Data A and Data B")
Coefficient Comparison Between Data A and Data B
Beta_1990s Beta_2000s
(Intercept) 2.6163205 2.9869736
age -0.0029420 -0.0038004
tumor_size -0.0493779 -0.0591790
lymph_nodes -0.0631821 -0.0314432
tumor_grade2 -0.0746786 0.0323765
tumor_grade3 -0.2281479 -0.2017433
ER1 0.2602014 0.2158351
HER21 -0.1749359 0.0364582
hormonal_therapy1 0.1366341 0.2144125 
cat("R Square:", r2_B)
## R Square: 0.1836521

The model does not generalize well. While the model still captures the general direction of most clinical risks, the significant increase in error and the dramatic shift in specific biological predictors (like HER2) suggest that a model trained on 1990s “medical reality” is no longer calibrated for the 2000s.

Assessment of Generalization

The prediction model built on the 1990–1995 cohort (datA) performs poorly when applied to the 2000–2005 cohort (datB). We justify this conclusion based on three key metrics:

  • Increased Error: The Mean Squared Error (MSE) rose from approximately 0.486 (during internal cross-validation) to 0.7058 in the external validation. This represents a nearly 45% increase in prediction error.
  • Reduced Explanatory Power: The \(R^2\) dropped from 0.214 to 0.1676, indicating that the 1990s model explains significantly less variance in the PFS of the 2000s patients.
  • Coefficient Instability: Several key coefficients shifted dramatically in magnitude and direction (see Table 1 in Appendix), proving that the “rules” governing breast cancer progression changed over that decade.

Changes in Prediction

By comparing the 1990s coefficients (\(\beta_{1990s}\)) with the newly trained 2000s coefficients (\(\beta_{2000s}\)), we can identify several critical shifts in breast cancer research and treatment:

  • The “HER2 Revolution” The most striking change is the HER2 status. In the 1990s, HER2-positive status was a strong negative prognostic factor (\(\beta = -0.1749\)). However, in the 2000s model, the coefficient flipped to slightly positive (\(\beta = 0.0365\)).

Clinical Interpretation: This shift is almost certainly due to the clinical introduction of Trastuzumab (Herceptin) in the late 1990s. HER2-positive patients, who previously had the worst outcomes, began receiving targeted therapy that effectively neutralized the biological risk of the HER2 protein, fundamentally altering the survival landscape.

  • Management of Nodal Disease The negative impact of Lymph Node involvement significantly decreased, with the coefficient moving from -0.0632 to -0.0314.

Clinical Interpretation: This suggests that systemic treatments (chemotherapy and newer targeted agents) in the early 2000s became more effective at treating micrometastatic disease, making the presence of positive lymph nodes less of a “death sentence” for PFS than it was a decade prior.

  • Potency of Hormonal Therapy The protective effect of Hormonal Therapy increased from 0.1366 to 0.2144.

Clinical Interpretation: This likely reflects the transition from Tamoxifen as the sole standard of care to the widespread use of Aromatase Inhibitors (AIs) (e.g., Letrozole, Anastrozole) for post-menopausal women, which clinical trials proved to be more effective at preventing recurrence.

  • Baseline Survival Improvement (The Intercept) The model Intercept increased from 2.616 to 2.987.

Clinical Interpretation: This “baseline” jump indicates a general improvement in progression-free survival across the board, likely due to better diagnostic imaging, earlier detection, and improved supportive care (e.g., managing treatment side effects).

Build a New Prediction model for the 2000-2005 cohort.

Choose linear terms and nonlinear terms

p1_b = ggplot(datB, aes(x = age, y = log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Age")
p2_b = ggplot(datB, aes(x = bmi, y = log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "BMI")
p3_b = ggplot(datB, aes(x = tumor_size, y = log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Tumor Size")
p4_b = ggplot(datB, aes(x = lymph_nodes, y = log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Lymph Nodes")
p5_b = ggplot(datB, aes(x = Ki67, y = log_PFS)) + plot_labs + labs(title = "Ki67")

(p1_b | p2_b) / (p3_b | p4_b) / p5_b +
  plot_annotation(title = "Plots for Selecting Linear and Nonlinear Terms in Data B")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Build GAM model

m1_b = gam(log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + 
             tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy, data = datB)

m2_b = gam(log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + menopause + race + 
             tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy, data = datB)

anova(m1_b, m2_b, test = "F")
## Analysis of Deviance Table
## 
## Model 1: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + 
##     race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + 
##     hormonal_therapy
## Model 2: log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + s(tumor_size) + lymph_nodes + s(Ki67) + 
##     menopause + race + tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + 
##     surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy
##   Resid. Df Resid. Dev     Df Deviance      F Pr(>F)
## 1    1977.1     874.35                              
## 2    1973.5     871.27 3.6523    3.087 1.9162 0.1116

Summary the model

gam_fit_B = gam(
  log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datB
)

summary(gam_fit_B)
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + 
##     ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy
## 
## Parametric coefficients:
##                    Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.986974   0.088965  33.575  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.003800   0.001334  -2.849 0.004436 ** 
## tumor_size        -0.059179   0.012883  -4.593 4.63e-06 ***
## lymph_nodes       -0.031443   0.008497  -3.701 0.000221 ***
## tumor_grade2       0.032377   0.038090   0.850 0.395422    
## tumor_grade3      -0.201743   0.041812  -4.825 1.51e-06 ***
## ER1                0.215835   0.049979   4.319 1.65e-05 ***
## HER21              0.036458   0.036232   1.006 0.314425    
## hormonal_therapy1  0.214412   0.046329   4.628 3.93e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Approximate significance of smooth terms:
##          edf Ref.df     F  p-value    
## s(bmi) 3.005  3.813 8.163 5.31e-06 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## R-sq.(adj) =  0.184   Deviance explained = 18.8%
## GCV = 0.44701  Scale est. = 0.44432   n = 2000

Compare GAM and LM models

set.seed(123)
folds_b = createFolds(datB$log_PFS, k = 10, list = TRUE)

cv_mse_lm_b  = numeric(10)
cv_mse_gam_b = numeric(10)

for(i in 1:10) {
  valid_idx = folds_b[[i]]
  train_b = datB[-valid_idx, ]
  test_b = datB[valid_idx, ]
  
  # LM
  lm_b = lm(log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy, data = train_b)
  # GAM
  gam_b = gam(log_PFS ~ age + s(bmi) + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + tumor_grade + ER + HER2 + hormonal_therapy, data = train_b)
  
  cv_mse_lm_b[i]  = mean((test_b$log_PFS - predict(lm_b, test_b))^2)
  cv_mse_gam_b[i] = mean((test_b$log_PFS - predict(gam_b, test_b))^2)
}

mse_lm_b = mean(cv_mse_lm_b)
print(mse_lm_b)
## [1] 0.4527149
mse_gam_b = mean(cv_mse_gam_b)
print(mse_gam_b)
## [1] 0.4465694

Build Ridge, LASSO, and MARS models

x_b = model.matrix(log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + 
                     tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy, data = datB)[, -1]
y_b = datB$log_PFS

# Ridge & Lasso
cv_lasso_b = cv.glmnet(x_b, y_b, alpha = 1)
cv_ridge_b = cv.glmnet(x_b, y_b, alpha = 0)

# MARS
mars_fit_b = train(
  log_PFS ~ age + bmi + tumor_size + lymph_nodes + Ki67 + menopause + race + 
    tumor_grade + ER + PR + HER2 + stage + surgery + chemo + hormonal_therapy,
  data = datB, method = "earth", tuneGrid = mars_grid, trControl = ctrl1
)

Compare models

pred_lasso = predict(cv_lasso_b, x_b, s = "lambda.min")
pred_ridge = predict(cv_ridge_b, x_b, s = "lambda.min")

mse_lasso_b = mean((y_b - pred_lasso)^2)
mse_ridge_b = mean((y_b - pred_ridge)^2)

mse_lasso_b
## [1] 0.444569
mse_ridge_b
## [1] 0.4456067
best_mse_mars_b = (mars_fit_b$results |> 
  filter(nprune == mars_fit_b$bestTune$nprune, degree == mars_fit_b$bestTune$degree) |>
  pull(RMSE))^2

best_mse_mars_b
## [1] 0.4512692
model_mse_b = data.frame(
  Model = c("LM", "GAM", "Ridge", "Lasso", "MARS"),
  MSE = c(mse_lm_b, mse_gam_b, mse_ridge_b, mse_lasso_b, best_mse_mars_b)) |>
    arrange(MSE)

pretty_table(model_mse_b, caption = "Model Comparison by MSE for Data B")
Model Comparison by MSE for Data B
Model MSE
Lasso 0.4445690
Ridge 0.4456067
GAM 0.4465694
MARS 0.4512692
LM 0.4527149

Comment: Lasso model achieved the lowest mean squared error, indicating the best performance, so we choose Lasso Regression.

coef_A = as.matrix(coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.min"))
coef_B = as.matrix(coef(cv_lasso_b, s = "lambda.min"))

lasso_comparison = data.frame(
  Predictor = rownames(coef_A),
  Beta_1990s = round(coef_A[, 1], 4),
  Beta_2000s = round(coef_B[, 1], 4)
)  |> 
  filter(Beta_1990s != 0 | Beta_2000s != 0) |>
  mutate(Status = case_when(
    Beta_1990s != 0 & Beta_2000s == 0 ~ "Dropped in 2000s",
    Beta_1990s == 0 & Beta_2000s != 0 ~ "New in 2000s",
    TRUE ~ "Kept in both"
  ))

pretty_table(lasso_comparison, caption = "Lasso Coefficient Comparison Across Cohorts")
Lasso Coefficient Comparison Across Cohorts
Predictor Beta_1990s Beta_2000s Status
(Intercept) (Intercept) 2.5722 3.1620 Kept in both
age age -0.0018 -0.0032 Kept in both
bmi bmi 0.0000 -0.0045 New in 2000s
tumor_size tumor_size -0.0476 -0.0595 Kept in both
lymph_nodes lymph_nodes -0.0597 -0.0290 Kept in both
Ki67 Ki67 -0.0040 -0.0053 Kept in both
menopause1 menopause1 0.0000 -0.0242 New in 2000s
race2 race2 0.0000 -0.0257 New in 2000s
race3 race3 0.0059 -0.0233 Kept in both
race4 race4 -0.0132 0.0855 Kept in both
tumor_grade2 tumor_grade2 0.0000 0.0804 New in 2000s
tumor_grade3 tumor_grade3 -0.1027 -0.0894 Kept in both
ER1 ER1 0.2249 0.1350 Kept in both
PR1 PR1 0.0000 0.0647 New in 2000s
HER21 HER21 -0.1428 0.0338 Kept in both
stage2 stage2 0.0000 0.0175 New in 2000s
surgery1 surgery1 0.0000 -0.0096 New in 2000s
chemo1 chemo1 0.0000 -0.0067 New in 2000s
hormonal_therapy1 hormonal_therapy1 0.1192 0.2061 Kept in both

Comment:

Significant Improvement in Baseline Survival: The model intercept increased substantially from 2.572 to 3.162. This jump indicates that, while controlling for all other clinical covariates (such as age and tumor characteristics), patients in the 2000s cohort exhibited a markedly higher baseline \(log(PFS)\) compared to those in the 1990s.

Diminishing Impact of Nodal Metastasis: The coefficient for lymph_nodes narrowed from -0.0597 to -0.0290. While positive lymph node involvement remains a negative prognostic indicator, its relative impact on disease progression was reduced by approximately 50% in the later cohort.

Emergence of New Metabolic and Physiological Predictors: Variables such as bmi and menopause1 (menopausal status), which were excluded from the 1990s model via Lasso regularization (coefficients reduced to zero), emerged as significant predictors in the 2000s model. The inclusion of bmi (-0.0045) and menopause1 (-0.0242) suggests a shift in the clinical landscape where metabolic health and hormonal status have become more critical in determining long-term outcomes.

Reversal of Demographic Prognostic Factors: Most notably, the coefficient for race4 (Hispanic) shifted from a negative indicator (-0.0132) to a positive protective factor (+0.0855). This reversal suggests a fundamental change in the survival outcomes for this demographic, which may be attributed to improved health equity, better access to specialized care, or enhanced therapeutic responses to updated treatment protocols during this decade.
